What's going on everyone? Welcome back to the Business Intelligence Financial Metrics portion of this training focused on service. So let's get jiggy with it. Sometimes people will skip over things that they feel are the basics and it's because they don't understand the basics that they aren't able to succeed. So don't skip over it and you will be all the wiser. So financial, net revenue. So this is a key piece to understand because total revenue is what you're collecting, right? But your net revenue is that revenue minus fees, refunds, charge backs. Now this may not seem significant to you but let me paint you a picture. So let's say you have a $500 sale. Now your fee is 3%. That's fairly standard for a lot. Some of them are more, some of them are less. We'll say 3%. Which means you have $15 now. So instead of receiving $500, you're not $485. On top of that, let's say that your refund percentage is 5%. So all the transactions you run every month refund. Which means that 5% of $500 is $25. So now we're down to $560. So you know that 1% of your bills get charged back. Now a charge back in my mind is a refund plus a $25 fee. So that $25 fee is 1%. So you'd be looking at $0.25 per transaction that would be added onto it. So you're looking at, I mean essentially the same number. But I like to show all the fees so that you can see what's involved in it. So what we once believed was a $500 sale is actually a $459.75 sale. Now you're like, well, why is it so important to nail down the difference there? Because the average gym runs an 8% to 12% margin. Not our gyms, but the average gym runs an 8% to 12% margin. And depending on where you're at within our process, that very well may be you. And so if you're running, I'm going to say 10% for math sake because it's easier, a 10% margin, what did we just knock off? If 10% of $550, and that means that the $50 was going to be our margin and we just got rid of 40 of it. Ah, right. That is why accounting net revenue, not total revenue received is so important. Mind you, a lot of the processors that you encounter don't take either of those things into account. You do need to take those things into account when you're calculating your financials. Next is overhead. All costs including reinvestment into the business. And so what I want to cover here is that some people are like, well, this was a reinvestment. You know, like I bought more equipment. I didn't have to do that. Blah, blah, blah, blah. Unless you are, basically for the purposes of everyone who's watching this, unless you are a mid-sized, large business, so you have over 100 employees, most of the reinvestment in the business is going to be day to day things that you are doing. If you're like, well, I want to improve the building. I want to renovate. Cool. Those are costs, period. Anything that does not go into your pocket that comes out of your business is a cost. So I don't want to be like, well, we made this much, but I only took home this, but I could have taken, like, I don't want to get into that math. It's fake. It's only to make you feel better. All costs, including reinvestment into the business. Information, seminars, everything. Rent, toilet paper, electricity, all the reinvestment, everything goes into this. The reason I do it that way is that it's much easier to calculate because all you have to do is look at your bank statement and then look at the credits, right? The withdrawals from your account, all the minuses. Look at the total. That is your over. And you can average it over the last three months. That is your over. All right? So be mindful of that. And then that way you can have a very accurate raw estimate of what your overhead is. Not where you add it up to be in your mind on an Excel sheet, but what it actually is. Total build. All transactions that are intended to run. So if you have 100 EFTs at $200 a month and all, you know, 100 of them run, that means they have the intention to build, but only, you know, 90 of them go through successfully, then that means that the initial 20 grand that we build is the total build. So it's everything that we intended to run. All right? I was almost getting into this one, which was failed transactions. Total failed transactions is the transactions intended to run that did not run for whatever reason. So in that situation where we had 100 that were intended to run and then 90 actually ran, 10, the difference is the total failed transactions. Total recovery transactions is of those 10 that did not run, how many of them did we recover? This is important because you can see if your billing team or your operations manager is slipping up. There's two reasons why recovery and getting the percentage of recovery is an important measure to track, which is this next one. And the reason it's important to track is so that, one, you know how much money you're actually receiving back. So like you should be getting that whole 10% back so you can see how hard they're working it. The second reason is that this will affect your term, how well your team goes out and collects this and collects the client, etc., will affect your term. Okay? That means that like your attrition will be directly related to how hard you recover, how well you do that. And the percentage recovery is simply the number of transactions or the money that you recovered over the percent over the amount of money that failed. So if you had a thousand that was recovered over 2000 that failed, you'd have a 50% recovery. Okay? Profit. You're like, I know what profit is. It's worth defining. Net revenue minus overhead. That is your profit. And use the net revenue that I give you and the overhead that I give you and then you'll see your real actual profit. Note for you, you should put yourself on payroll for fair market price for being a general manager if you do not have one. So if you do not have a manager in your business, you are the manager. And so you should give yourself, typically, depends on the market, check your account and legal compliance, whatever, but you should typically pay yourself $30,000 to $40,000 a year out of payroll because that is what the fair market value for the job that you were doing is. Anything in excess of that would be true profit to the business. So if you were to sell it, the owner, if someone was to come in and buy it and you wanted to continue to run the gym, then you would get paid for continuing to run the gym and then that person would get paid based on the profit that it was creating in excess of that role. That's why it's important to delineate what you were getting paid for doing the job versus what the business is profiting as a business in excess of the model. If you're like, well, there is no profit besides my thing, then that means that we need to make adjustments to the business model, which is why we're doing this whole thing to be doing it. Last one here is profit margin percentage, which is simply put your profit divided by your net revenue. So if you profited $1,000 and your net revenue was $20,000, then you had to have a 5% profit margin. That is how this works. And then noting that when you start marking those things, you will be able to focus on them and whatever gets measured gets managed and that's how we can improve it. Woo, finances! Just so I don't forget, these last two here are refunds and percentage refunded. Refunds are transactions that you reverse. So once it does successfully bill and then you reverse that transaction, that is a refund. Percentage refunded is you're going to take the total amount of refunds if you add them all together and you divide that by the total revenue. Mind you, at the top here we talked about net revenue. That had already taken out the refunds, but for you to understand what percentage of your revenue is being refunded will help you factor in what you're pricing your products at. So if you want to make X on a sale, then you need to factor in what your refund percentage is. And when you're doing your calculations, you should know that 5%, 6%, 7%, whatever the percentage is, is being refunded. So when you're calculating how much money you'd be making for a particular service or product or whatever, then you want to take that off the top. If 10% of people are refunding a thousand dollar product, then it means that every thousand dollar sale you're really only making 900. Does that make sense? Hopefully that makes sense. So I didn't have this in the original layout, but I think it's worth mentioning to you here. But you should try it.
